title: Hooks, Configuration, Credentials, and Repository Trust · Repository Trace: Gita Safe Directory Ownership
summary: A cloned repository proposes hooks, includeIf config, credential helpers, aliases, filters, safe.directory exceptions, and a maintenance schedule on a shared build host. Planned artifact: Add and verify the gita safe directory ownership increment in repository trust and local automation threat model. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Explain protected configuration and safe.directory ownership exceptions without using broad wildcards to conceal unsafe shared repositories.
Inspect configuration origin, scope, type, includes, conditional includes, worktree config, environment, and command overrides before trusting behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.shell:
# git-advanced cumulative repository evidence notebook
# Git operations change only disposable simulated repository state.
# Inspect status, diffs, graph, refs, and recovery before every mutation.

steps.title: Predict Gita Safe Directory Ownership
steps.instruction:
theory case — Case G37 is a theory in a disposable repository. conditional user configuration injects a credential helper and hooks path for this repository. The learner must predict scope and origin identify the exact protected or local source of each effective value, run git config --show-origin --show-scope --get-regexp "^(core|credential|include|safe)\.", explain a global includeIf sets credential.helper while local core.hooksPath points into repository data, test this changed condition: change repository ownership and refuse a wildcard safe.directory exception, and preserve this recovery boundary: capture effective config, remove narrow trust grants, and rotate any exposed credential.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Gita Safe Directory Ownership contract for the supplied data.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for explain protected configuration and safe.directory ownership exceptions without using broad wildcards to conceal unsafe shared repositories.

content.title: Reasoning move
content.text: Locate repository and operation state, compare working tree, index, refs, and graph, preserve a recovery reference, preview the smallest change, then verify both tree behavior and history topology.

content.title: Worked case pattern
content.text: Case G37 is a worked example in a disposable repository. conditional user configuration injects a credential helper and hooks path for this repository. The learner must predict scope and origin identify the exact protected or local source of each effective value, run git config --show-origin --show-scope --get-regexp "^(core|credential|include|safe)\.", explain a global includeIf sets credential.helper while local core.hooksPath points into repository data, test this changed condition: change repository ownership and refuse a wildcard safe.directory exception, and preserve this recovery boundary: capture effective config, remove narrow trust grants, and rotate any exposed credential. A defensible solution applies Gita Safe Directory Ownership, preserves the governing invariant or value contract, and connects the observed result to repository trust and local automation threat model.

content.code:
# Advanced Git 2.55 deterministic scenario 1
# before-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# hypothesis: conditional user configuration injects a credential helper and hooks path for this repository
git config --show-origin --show-scope --get-regexp "^(core|credential|include|safe)\."
# expected: scope and origin identify the exact protected or local source of each effective value
# observation: a global includeIf sets credential.helper while local core.hooksPath points into repository data
# changed-case: change repository ownership and refuse a wildcard safe.directory exception
# after-ref: main=a11ce00 candidate=b00b1e5
# tree-proof: compare the exact affected tree and paths before judging success
# behavior-proof: retain a changed-case test or invariant that can falsify the Git-only conclusion
# recovery: capture effective config, remove narrow trust grants, and rotate any exposed credential
# transfer-limit: inspect unknown repositories in isolation with hooks, filters, aliases, and external helpers disabled
content.caption: Runnable repository-state trace example; predict its result before running, then change one boundary input.

steps.title: Build Gita Safe Directory Ownership evidence
steps.instruction: Append an Advanced Git evidence block headed "# Evidence: gita-safe-directory-ownership-jpawp2" with exact before and after refs; a falsifiable prediction; the bounded simulator command; expected, observed, tree, behavior, changed-case, and recovery evidence; and one honest real-repository transfer limit.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Gita Safe Directory Ownership into observable performance evidence.

content.code:
# Evidence: gita-safe-directory-ownership-jpawp2
# scenario-key: trace-jpawp2 probe-2pwapj boundary-awp2jp owner-jpawp2x fault-jpawp2z
# before-ref: main=a11cejp candidate=b00b1p2
# hypothesis: conditional user configuration injects a credential helper and hooks path for this repository
git config --show-origin --show-scope --get-regexp "^(core|credential|include|safe)\."
# expected: scope and origin identify the exact protected or local source of each effective value
# observation: a global includeIf sets credential.helper while local core.hooksPath points into repository data
# changed-case: change repository ownership and refuse a wildcard safe.directory exception
# after-ref: main=a11cejp candidate=c0ffee2
# tree-proof: the affected tree and path set match the predicted repository transition
# behavior-proof: a changed input preserves the named product or release invariant
# recovery: capture effective config, remove narrow trust grants, and rotate any exposed credential
# transfer-limit: inspect unknown repositories in isolation with hooks, filters, aliases, and external helpers disabled
